coredata.dc:title: Multicolor emission-based nitrogen, sulfur and boron co-doped photoluminescent carbon dots for sequential sensing of Fe3+ and cysteine: RGB color sensor and live cell imaging

coredata.dc:description: Herein, a simple hydrothermal synthesis is used to prepare multiple heteroatom-doped photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) from thiourea (N and S source) and boric acid (B source) as precursors. The optical and physicochemical properties of the as-synthesized NSB-CDs were studied using UV–Vis, photoluminescence, TEM, FT-IR, XRD, Raman, and XPS analyses. The NSB-CDs exhibited excellent stability, high photostability, pH, and ionic strength tolerance; they retained their excellent stability independent of excitation. The NSB-CDs featured small sizes of approximately 3.2 ± 0.4 nm (range: 2.0–5.0 nm) as evidenced using TEM measurements. The NSB-CDs were used as a photoluminescent sensing platform to detect Fe3+ as well as cysteine (Cys) molecules. The competitive binding of Cys to Fe3+ resulted in NSB-CDs that retained their photoluminescence. For the rapid identification and quantification of Fe3+ and Cys, NSB-CDs were developed as a “switch-on” dual-function sensing platform. The linear detection range of Fe3+ was 0–20 μM (limit of detection [LOD]: 54.4 nM) and that of Cys was 0–50 μM (LOD: 4.9 nM). We also introduced a smartphone RGB analysis method for detecting low-concentration solutions based on digital images. The NSB-CDs showed no toxicity at 100 μg/mL. Photoluminescent probes for multicolor live-cell imaging can be used with NSB-CDs at this concentration, suggesting that NSB-CDs may be promising photoluminescent probes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption and PL spectrum of NSB-CDs (5 μg/mL) dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4) (λex = 450 nm; λex = 524 nm). Inset: Photographs of NSB-CDs under visible and 365 nm UV illumination. (b) Excitation-independent PL spectra of the NSB-CDs at varied excitation wavelengths. (c) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of the NSB-CDs. (d) Particle size distribution histograms of the NSB-CDs. (e) FT-IR and (f) XRD pattern of the NSB-CDs.
XPS spectra of the NSB-CDs (a) Survey scan, (b) B1s, (c) N1s, (d) S2p, (e) C1s, and (f) O1s.

(a) UV–Visible and (b) PL spectra of sensor NSB-CDs (5 μg/mL) with the addition of various cations (25 μM) at pH 7.4. (c) PL color changes of NSB-CDs (5 μg/mL) under UV illumination (365 nm) with the addition of cations (25 μM) at pH 7.4.

(a) PL response of NSB-CDs (5 μg/mL) following the addition of Fe3+ (0–25 μM). Inset: Photographs of NSB-CDs and NSB-CDs/Fe3+ under 365 nm UV light. (b) The relationship between PL intensities and the concentration of Fe3+. (c) Job’s plot for determining the stoichiometry of NSB-CDs (5 μg/mL) with Fe3+ in 10 mM HEPES buffer solution at pH 7.4. (d) Calibration curve of NSB-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ using the monitored emission wavelength at 513 nm. Excitation wavelength: 450 nm.

(a) PL response of NSB-CDs (5 μg/mL) with Fe3+ (25 μM) following the addition of Cys (0–50 μM). Insets: Photographs of NSB-CDs/Fe3+ and NSB-CDs/Fe3++Cys under 365 nm UV illumination. (b) B–H plot between the relative PL signal 1/(P − P0) and Cys. (c) Job’s plot for determining the stoichiometry of NSB-CDs/Fe3+ with Cys in 10 mM HEPES buffer solution at pH 7.4. (d) Calibration curve of NSB-CDs/Fe3+ in the presence of Cys using the monitored emission wavelength at 531 nm. Excitation wavelength: 450 nm.
Color information (RGB values) of the photoluminescent probe (NSB-CDs, NSB-CDs/Fe3+, and NSB-CDs/Fe3++Cys) is obtained using a smartphone application (Color recognizer, RGB color picker).

(a) Cell viability using HCT-116 cells were cultured with the addition of 0–100 μg/mL of NSB-CDs. (b) Fluorescence images of HCT-116 cells treated with NSB-CDs, NSB-CDs/Fe3+, and NSB-CDs/Fe3++Cys. Phase contrast, fluorescence (λex = 450 nm), and merged fluorescence images are shown.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a simple hydrothermal synthesis is used to prepare multiple heteroatom-doped photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) from thiourea (N and S source) and boric acid (B source) as precursors. The optical and physicochemical properties of the as-synthesized NSB-CDs were studied using UV–Vis, photoluminescence, TEM, FT-IR, XRD, Raman, and XPS analyses. The NSB-CDs exhibited excellent stability, high photostability, pH, and ionic strength tolerance; they retained their excellent stability independent of excitation. The NSB-CDs featured small sizes of approximately 3.2 ± 0.4 nm (range: 2.0–5.0 nm) as evidenced using TEM measurements. The NSB-CDs were used as a photoluminescent sensing platform to detect Fe3+ as well as cysteine (Cys) molecules. The competitive binding of Cys to Fe3+ resulted in NSB-CDs that retained their photoluminescence. For the rapid identification and quantification of Fe3+ and Cys, NSB-CDs were developed as a “switch-on” dual-function sensing platform. The linear detection range of Fe3+ was 0–20 μM (limit of detection [LOD]: 54.4 nM) and that of Cys was 0–50 μM (LOD: 4.9 nM). We also introduced a smartphone RGB analysis method for detecting low-concentration solutions based on digital images. The NSB-CDs showed no toxicity at 100 μg/mL. Photoluminescent probes for multicolor live-cell imaging can be used with NSB-CDs at this concentration, suggesting that NSB-CDs may be promising photoluminescent probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials, which are mostly composed of carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene, and carbon nanofilms, are promising scaffolds with diameters smaller than 10 nm and have been essential components in several applications, including biological markers, biochemistry, and biomedicine [1]. Over the last decades, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted substantial interest and their potential as fluorescent materials is expected to increase due to low toxicity and tunable surface functional groups [2,3]. Various CD fabrication methods have been developed including electrochemical oxidation processes [4], arc discharge [5], hydrothermal [6–10], chemical oxidation [11], and combustion [12]. Hetero-atoms, such as chlorine (Cl) nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), boron (B), oxygen (O), and phosphorus (P), have been shown to increase the optical properties of CDs [13,14]. In addition to other heteroatom dopants, N, S, and B are considered the most effective. Consequently, CDs exhibit improved fluorescence quantum yields, structural properties, and surface morphologies [13–16]. Carbon surfaces with multiple active surfaces offer simultaneous catalytic properties and multiple active sites. Multiple co-doping of heteroatoms on CDs is an increasingly attractive option since it can lead to a distinctive electronic structure due to the synergistic effect of the doped heteroatoms [13–16].

Trace metals and amino acids that contain thiols play unique roles in biological processes [17]. An invaluable trace element in organisms is Fe3+, which is crucial in metabolic processes [18]. Cysteine (Cys) is one of the richest intracellular biothiols, with numerous functions, including metal binding, detoxification, protein synthesis, and metabolism [19]. Cys deficiency can lead to slow growth in children, edema, hair depigmentation, weakness, lethargy, liver damage, body lesions, fat loss, and other health problems [20]. This has led to a growing interest in developing fluorescent chemosensors to detect Cys. In biological systems, Cys plays a key role in maintaining the physiological redox balance, and overabundance or absence of Cys can lead to several diseases (including anemia, diabetes, heart failure, and cardiovascular disease) [20,21]. Thus, imbalances must be promptly identified and quantified. Currently, Fe3+ and Cys can be detected quantitatively using a variety of methods [22–29]. However, eliminating some limitations of these methods, such as the need for enormous and expensive instrumentation, long analysis times, and offline monitoring, remains a challenge. This is despite the use of these techniques to measure Fe3+ and Cys. In this context, fluorescence methods are of considerable interest due to their simplicity, selectivity, and sensitivity as well as fast response time and ability to screen Fe3+ and Cys on time. Research on organic molecules, nanomaterials, drug interactions, and sensing materials has been a priority for our group and focuses on other work over the last decade [30–41].

In the present study, thiourea is N/S source, and boric acid is B source to prepare novel NSB-CDs using a hydrothermal method. Because of their high photostability and solubility, NSB-CDs exhibited strong green PL. In addition, the prepared NSB-CDs were characterized using UV–Vis and PL emission spectroscopy, TEM, FTIR, XRD, Raman, and XPS techniques. As evidenced by the quenching effect, the aqueous solution of NSB-CDs exhibited selective sensitivity to Fe3+, and the quenched PL was recovered by adding Cys, which is a strong chelating agent. To the best of our knowledge, label-free detection of Fe3+ and Cys using multiple-heteroatom doping has not been reported. In addition to its ability to detect intracellular Cys and Fe3+ efficiently, the method is highly biocompatible and can be further used for cell imaging.

Boric acid (H3BO3) and thiourea (CH4N2S) were purchased from the TCI Chemical Company, South Korea. A stock solution of NaCl (1.0 mol/L) was prepared. A solution of phosphoric acid (H3PO4) or sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was added to prepare solutions with different pH values.

The NSB-CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal process [6–9]. Typically, 0.618 g of boric acid and 0.761 g of thiourea were mixed in a 50 mL aqueous solution under magnetic stirring for 30 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred into an 80 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h in an oven. To remove agglomerated particles, the aqueous suspensions were allowed to cool at room temperature and filtered using 0.22 μm filter membranes. After centrifugation, the product was collected, and the supernatants were stored at 4 °C until further characterization.

The intensity was determined using a PL spectrum to study the sensing range of NSB-CDs for cations. Solutions that contained various metal ions (Ag+, Ce3+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Ni2+, Ti3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Na+, and Fe3+) were systematically mixed with NSB-CDs. NSB-CD (5 μg/mL) and 1.97 mL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4) were mixed with various content (0–25 μM) of Fe3+. Similarly, Fe3+ solutions were added to cations (50 μM) or amino acids (50 μM; Ala, Asp, Cys, GSH, Gly, His, Leu, Met, Pro, Phe, Ser, Try, Trp, and Tyr) to evaluate anti-interference effects. The PL intensity of the NSB-CD/Fe3+ system was measured after 50 μL of various content of Cys (0–50 μM) were mixed with 1.95 mL of NSB-CD solution.

The optical properties were examined and the UV–Vis and PL spectra of NSB-CD at room temperature are shown in Fig. 1 a,b. Two UV–Vis absorption peaks with centers at 245 and 293 nm are present; they are explained by the π–π*transition of aromatic sp2 domains [42] (Fig. 1a). The 450 nm absorption peaks on the surface of the NSB-CDs are probably due to CO/CN transitions [43]. The NSB-CDs were well dispersed; the solution appeared transparent under visible light, and NSB-CDs exhibited intense green luminescence under UV irradiation (Fig. 1a, inset). The maximum PL emission/excitation wavelengths were between 524/450 nm, with a range of excitation wavelengths between 330 and 500 nm (Fig. 1b). Using quinine sulfate as a standard, we calculated the fluorescence QY of the NSB-CDs to be approximately 27.5 %. Additionally, the NSB-CDs exhibited good PL stability (above 90 %) when stored at 4 °C in the dark for ∼6 months (Fig. S1). The stability of the NSB-CDs has been investigated under several variables, including UV light irradiation, solvents, pH, and ionic strength [44]. Under continuous 365 nm UV light irradiation, the PL intensity of the NSB-CDs was not significantly changed by polar or nonpolar solvents in preliminary experiments (Fig. S2a), ultrahigh concentration (10 M) of NaCl solution (Fig. S2b), and a wide range of pH values (1–13, Fig. S2c). Photographs of the photostability of the various solvents, salt ionic strength, color variation, and stability in a wide range of pH under daylight and UV light irradiations are shown in Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of the NSB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological and structural characterization of the NSB-CDs were performed using TEM, FTIR, XRD, and XPS analyses, as shown in Fig. 1c–f. As shown in Fig. 1c, there was no apparent aggregation of NSB-CDs in the water, and their shape was almost spherical [45]. A high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 1c) reveals 0.21-nm lattice fringes on the NSB-CDs, corresponding to the graphitic carbon (100) plane [46]. The statistical analysis of over 50 particles revealed a relatively narrow size distribution of NSB-CDs, between 2 and 5 nm, with an average diameter of ∼3.2 nm (Fig. 1d). Functional groups in NSB-CDs have been identified using FT-IR spectroscopy [8,10,16]. The FTIR spectral analysis of the NSB-CDs is presented in Fig. 1e.

The peak at 3617 cm−1 is associated with the inner OH stretching vibration and the peak at 3457 cm−1 with the outer NH stretching vibration [8]. Moreover, the peaks at 1651, 1548, 1399, and 1108 cm−1 can be ascribed to CO, CC, and CH2. The 850 cm−1 resembles NH bending [16]. The O–H, N–H, and CO surface functional groups to the high-water solubility of the NSB-CDs. The BOH bending/deformation vibration, N-B-O, and B-N are likely in agreement with the apparent absorption bands at 1198, 1032, 1099, and 779 cm−1, respectively [8,10,16]. XRD measurements supported the crystalline character of the NSB-CDs. The XRD pattern of the NSB-CDs in Fig. 1f displays a large diffraction peak cantered at 2θ = 24.6° that is attributable to the graphitic carbon(002) lattice spacing, indicating the presence of structures resembling graphite. Raman spectra agreement strong confirmation of B-doping in the graphene lattice. The Raman spectrum for the NSB-CDs (Fig. S4) displayed two broad peaks at 1367 and 1558 cm−1 which correspond to the D (sp3 hybrid carbon) and G (sp2 hybrid carbon) bands, respectively. The more intensive G bands and less intensive D bands represented the characteristic graphitic and defect lattice vibration. The intensity ratio of ID/IG value was calculated to be 0.95 indicating the structural and intrinsic defects and amorphous disorder for NSB-CDs. These results indicate that the graphitic core and surface-related state co-existed in the as-prepared NSB-CDs.

Additionally, the surface elements and chemical bonds of the NSB-CDs were analyzed using XPS. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the XPS spectrum of the NSB-CD shows five strong peaks at 192.6, 400.1, 168.6, 284.3, and 531.6 eV that belong to B1s, N1s, S2p, C1s, and O1s [8,10,16]. The elemental contents of B1s, N1s, S2p, C1s, and O1s were estimated as 14.03, 21.82, 18.14, 37.36, and 8.65 %, respectively (Table S1). The B1s spectrum of the NSB-CDs (Fig. 2b) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 190.4, 191.2, and 192.3 eV, which were accredited to BC, BO, and BS groups, respectively [8]. The three different carbon states, CNC, NC, and NH can be found at 399.6, 401.3, and 401.9 eV, respectively, in the N1s expanded spectrum (Fig. 2c).

Three peaks appeared at 168.2, 168.8, and 169.8 eV seen in the S2p XPS spectra (Fig. 2d), and they were assigned to the S2p3/2, S2p1/2, and SO groups, respectively [10]. Fig. 2e demonstrates four peaks at 283.8, 284.7, 285.5, and 286.2 eV, which correspond to the CB, CC/CO, CO/CS, and CN/CO bonds [8,10,16]. Further separation was observed in the spectrum of O1s at 531.0, 531.7, and 532.6 eV (Fig. 2f) owing to the presence of CO, CO, and OCO groups. Thus, in agreement with the FTIR results, the surface components of the NSB-CDs were determined using XPS. N, S, and B were effectively incorporated into the CDs, and their related functional groups, including amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl, were present.

A correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9600 was calculated within the linear content from 0 to 13 μM. A Job plot was used to investigate the complexation ratio of the NSB-CDs with Fe3+. Fig. 4c, the PL intensity at 524 nm can be interpreted using the molar ratio of [Fe3+] to [Fe3++NSB-CDs]. The maximum PL intensity was repeatedly observed to occur at a molar ratio of 0.5. Based on this result, Fe3+ is bound to NSB-CDs at a 1:1 ratio. The detection limit (LOD = K × SD/S) was 54.4 nM,and PL intensity was linearly related to Fe3+ content in the range of 0 and 25 μM (Fig. 4d), which is comparable to or even better than those reported in previous literature given in Table S2.

The detection threshold was substantially lower than the drinking water standard set by the Environmental Protection Agency (5.37 μM) [51]. To measure the selectivity of the chemosensor NSB-CDs, competition studies were conducted, in which the NSB-CDs were placed in a solution of Fe3+ with the addition of other distinct metal ions, including Ag+, Ce3+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Ni2+, Ti3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Sr2+, and Na+. As shown in Figs. S5 and S6, after the addition of different metal ions at the same amount (50 μM) and excitation at450 nm, PL intensity, and the accompanying color changed noticeably. In the presence of competing cations, the slight decrease in PL intensity of the emission of the NSB-CD/Fe3+ combination was more significant. This finding demonstrates that the addition of typical metal ions has no impact on the specific detection of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Cys by the NSB-CD/Fe3+ complex
ce:section.ce:para: Among biothiols, Cys is the most significant, while Fe3+ is one of the most important trace elements, crucial to human metabolism [52]. Considering that Cys binds to Fe3+ with a remarkable affinity, we created a switch-on PL sensing platform to accurately detect Cys. The PL intensity of the NSB-CDs and NSB-CD/Fe3+ prolonged quickly in the pH range of 5–7 as shown in Fig. S7a, and then gradually declined in the pH range of 9–13. Hydroxyl and amine groups were protonated with the increasing pH in the range of acidic conditions, resulting in positive-charged NSB-CDs [53]. In NSB-CDs, hydroxyl group protonation is slowly replaced by deprotonation as the pH increases, resulting in the formation of negatively charged hydroxyl groups that attract Fe3+ with Cys [54]. To demonstrate the validity of the suggested displacement strategy, the PL behavior of the NSB-CD/Fe3+ complex was examined toward various amino acids (Ala, Asp, Cys, GSH, Gly, His, Leu, Met, Pro, Phe, Ser, Try, Trp, and Tyr) and the interference was tested. As shown in Fig. S7a, PL intensity increased rapidly between pH 1 and 7. In the pH range of 9–13, PL intensity finally reached a constant level. After adding 25 μMof Fe3+ for 2 min, the PL of the NSB-CDs increased to its maximum value, and it recovered after adding 50 μMof Cys (Fig. S7b). Thus, the NSB-CD probe quickly detected Cys and Fe3+. Moreover, these interferents increased PL recovery, and Cys at the same amount (50 µM) increased PL intensity by nearly 12-foldwith consequent color change, demonstrating the remarkable specificity and sensitivity of the sensor probe (Figs. S8 and S9). Notably, the entire procedure can be completed in less than 10 s (Video 2), highlighting the sensitivity and effectiveness of the sensing platform. Therefore, we infer from the aforementioned data that Cys can be used to specifically differentiate NSB-CDs/Fe3+ based on the turn-on status of the resulting complexes [55,56].

The PL intensity of the solution of the NSB-CD/Fe3++Cys complex largely recovered as the Cys amount increased from 0 to 50 µM, which we believe can be attributed to the significant binding preference of SH toward Fe3+, as demonstrated in Fig. 5 a [55,56]. Further evidence for the PL turn-on with the NSB-CD/Fe3++Cys solution can be obtained from the finding that the sensor solutionemission color changed from yellow to a vivid yellowish green (inset in Fig. 5a). Moreover, a solid linear association between the concentration of Cys and the recovery efficiency 1/[P − P0] was found (R2 = 0.9972). (Fig. 5b). We generated a Job plot to investigate the complexation ratio between NSB-CDs/Fe3+ and Cys in more detail. The PL intensity at 541 nm versus the molar ratio [Cys]/[Cys] + [NSB − CDs/Fe3+] is shown in Fig. 5c. A molar ratio of 0.5 repeatedly produced the highest PL intensity. This suggests that Cys binds to NSB-CDs/Fe3+ at a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio. According to Fig. 5d, LOD is 4.9 nM (K × SD/S), and the PL intensity is directly related to Cys contentin the range between 0 and 50 μM, which is comparable to or even better than those reported in previous literature given in Table S3.

Fe3+ and Cys were added in that order, as shown in Fig. S10, resulting in a turn “off-on” switching of the PL response. The added Fe3+ and Cys to the NSB-CD aqueous solution caused NSB-CDs to demonstrate a “turn on–off-on” mode. The PL intensity of the NSB-CDs decreased when Fe3+ was added, which could be attributed to Fe3+-S bonds forming between the S-containing groups of NSB-CDs. The PL intensity of the NSB-CDs recoveredwhen Cys was added to the NSB-CD/Fe3+ combination, which may have been the result of a stronger binding relationship between Cys and Fe3+, allowing Fe3+ to be desorbed from the surface of the NSB-CDs. NSB-CDs can switch between different photoluminescence emission states in response to both Fe3+ and Cys chemical inputs, namely on (1) or off (0). With binary logic, it is possible to demonstrate the behavior of this molecular switch. Fig. S10b illustrates the input and output strings of a molecular switch with reversible 'On–Off' switching of two inputs (Fe3+ and Cys). The output signal is '1′ (strong emission) when both input strings '00 and 11′ are on (in the absence or presence of both Fe3+ and Cys), but '0′ (quenched emission), when only one of the two inputs Fe3+ and Cys is on. As a result of two chemical inputs, these photoluminescence changes mimic the behavior of an XNOR logic gate. Furthermore, the QY of NSB-CD/Fe3+ solution in the absence and presence of Cys was also measured. The results showed that the QY increased from 8.2 to 21.5 %, which supports the potential of NSB-CD/Fe3++Cys in bio-imaging applications. Zeta potentials were measured to validate the predicted interaction of NSB-CDs/Fe3+ and NSB-CDs/Fe3+ + Cys, as shown in Fig. S11. When Fe3+ is added to NSB-CDs solution, the zeta potential rises dramatically from −21.8 to +3.4 mV, owing to the expenditure of hydroxyl and amine groups and demonstrating the effective interaction of Fe3+. As demonstrated in Fig. S11, Cys causes the zeta potential of NSB-CDs/Fe3+ to shift from +3.4 to −16.7 mV, owing to the dissociation of Fe3+ from the surface of the NSB-CDs and verifying the chelation between Cys and Fe3+. According to the results, the association between NSB-CDs and Fe3+ is entirely reversible and can be reused repeatedly without suffering significant emission loss.

The use of smartphones for digital image-based chromogenic analyte detection is becoming increasingly popular because it simplifies data collection and analysis [57]. Color sensors provide a practical and reliable method to assess color changes and identify trace levels in this aspect [58,59]. A smartphone application (RGB color picker)analyzes colors using RGB data with 8-bit resolution. Image files often use a collection of red (R), green (G), and blue (B) constituents with varying pixel intensities to represent color. Every color componentintensity is expressed by a value between 0 and 255, where “0″ denotes dark intensity and ”255″ denotes brightness. The NSB-CD, NSB-CD/Fe3+, and NSB-CD/Fe3++Cysaqueous solutions, captured using a Samsung Galaxy S7 edgecamera, demonstrate that PL was generated in the cuvettes under UV illumination at 365 nm. The RGB color selector application, installed on the smartphone, was used to assess the RGB color values of the NSB-CD, NSB-CD/Fe3+, and NSB-CD/Fe3++Cys pictures, and theresults were instantly accessible. The NSB-CD, NSB-CD/Fe3+, and NSB-CD/Fe3++Cys aqueous solutions were bright green, yellow, and yellowish green, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6
[58,59] and had RGB values of (133, 214, 158), (201, 222, 19), and (196, 251, 95), respectively [60]. Thus, the RGB color selector application, which was used to recognize the RGB values of the NSB-CD, NSB-CD/Fe3+, and NSB-CD/Fe3++Cys aqueous solutions, enables a straightforward and on-site visual analysis.

Bioimaging and cell viability assays are indispensable for further applications of NSB-CDs [61]. The WST assay was used in the HCT-116 cell line to evaluate the ability of the NSB-CD sensor to detect Fe3+ and Cys in biological systems. A series of concentrations of the chemosensor NSB-CDs (12.5, 25, 37.5, 50, 62.5, 75, 87.5, and 100 μg/mL) was added to HCT-116 cells for 24 h, as shown in Fig. 7 a. HCT-116 cells were not harmed by the NSB-CD application, as shown in Fig. 7a. Under our experimental conditions, the NSB-CDs exhibited minimal cytotoxicity, as more than 94 % of the cells were viable [8,10]. Thus, the NSB-CDs were found to be of low toxicity to the cells, suggesting that they can be used in further bioimaging experiments. HCT-116 cells were incubated with NSB-CDs (5 μg/mL) for 15 min at 37 °C, rinsed thrice with PBS, and examined using confocal laser scanning microscopy to test the viability of cellular imaging. Free NSB-CDs displayed highly visible fluorescence signals in live cells in the absence of Fe3+ and Cys (Fig. 7b). However, following additional incubation with Fe3+, live cells showed a mild yellow fluorescence, which drastically diminished [62] (Fig. 7b). In HCT-116 cells containing the NSB-CD/Fe3++Cys complex formed by Fe3+ and the chemosensor NSB-CDs [63,64], fluorescent activity was restored by adding a Cys solution (Fig. 7b). The fluorescence signals on the chemosensor NSB-CDs were observed in the intracellular space when phase-contrast images were overlaid. This indicates that the chemosensor NSB-CDs have high membrane permeability. It was concluded from these results that the as-prepared NSB-CDs can be used as effective fluorescence sensors in biological systems to detect Fe3+ and Cys.

In summary, we synthesized multiple heteroatom-doped CDs (NSB-CDs) employing a straightforward hydrothermal method using thiourea as N/S source and boric acid as a B source. The resultant NSB-CDs displayed excellent fluorescence properties with a QY as high as 25.7 %. The obtained NSB-CDs exhibited good photostability and storage stability and had excellent optical and chemical properties. The observed excitation wavelength-dependent PL emission may be due to the narrow size distribution of the NSB-CDs, with small particle sizes of 3.2 ± 0.4 nm, as well as the high crystallinity of the NSB-CDs. The FT-IR and XPS results revealed that the surface of the CDs was functionalized with multiple heteroatoms (N, S, and B). In the presence of Fe3+ in the probe, the NSB-CDs showed large PL quenching and were highly selective towards Fe3+ over competing metal ions. Subsequently, PL could be recovered in the presence of Cys. The LOD of the chemosensor NSB-CDs for Fe3+ was 54.4 nM in the linear detection range of 0–25 μM and an LOD of 4.9 nM in the linear range of 0–50 μM for Cys was achieved. Furthermore, the developed sensor showed excellent biocompatibility, high selectivity, and minimal cytotoxicity, and could be used to successfully image intracellular Cys and Fe3+. Finally, novel insight was gained into the process of hydrothermal synthesis for producing biocompatible, highly sensitive, and cheap materials with controlled physical and chemical properties.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Naushad Ahmad: Validation. Mohammad Rizwan Khan: Methodology, Investigation. Kuppu Sakthi Velu: Methodology, Investigation. Karuppiah Kalaiselvi: Methodology, Investigation. Subramanian Palanisamy: Methodology, Investigation. SangGuan You: Methodology, Investigation. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
